communityVoices.quote: Can't think of a better He's Gone with an outstanding jam.
ain't it ironic that the only ever Jack Straw from Wichita (or maybe there are more and I just ain't looked hard enough?) in Wichita is cut/flawed/f*cked!!!!!
communityVoices.attribution: Tidewater four ten O nine, on Jack Straw

communityVoices.quote: Keith in particular is killing it during the jams. So great to hear him as a key melodic inventor of that nights music.
an very exploratory Other One featuring some great jazz piano work by Keith
Beautiful tone on Garcia's guitars as we soar above the golden wheat fields of Kansas.

whyThisShow: This is a late-fall 1972 soundboard that slipped past a lot of collectors — one reviewer noted it "escaped my collection some how" [source: 'This Wichita show escaped my collection some how'] — but the community that found it gave it five stars across the board. The second set is the draw: He's Gone opens into what one listener called "one of the finest post Gone thematic jams I've heard in the song" [source: 'one of the finest post Gone thematic jams I\'ve heard in the song'], and it drops directly into a Truckin' > Other One that runs roughly twenty minutes of exploratory territory. Keith Godchaux is a consistent talking point, with one reviewer describing him as "a key melodic inventor of that nights music" [source: 'a key melodic inventor of that nights music']. The first set holds up too, playing at what one listener called "virtually flawless, on a technical level" [source: 'The playing in the first set is virtually flawless, on a technical level'].
listenFor: The He's Gone is the centerpiece: one reviewer clocked Garcia taking over around the ten-minute mark and making "every second of the next 3 minutes count before it slows down to drop nicely into" Truckin' [source: 'Garcia makes every second of the next 3 minutes count before it slows down to drop nicely into']. The Other One then goes somewhere between spacey and exploratory, and Keith takes a notable solo around the twelve-minute mark before Garcia scales up behind him — one reviewer wrote "Keith wasn't ready to just turn it over and they have a nice moment" [source: 'Keith wasn\'t ready to just turn it over and they have a nice moment']. Bird Song in the first set drew a comparison to Veneta 8-27-72 from one listener [source: 'One of the Best Bird Song\'s ever (right up there with Veneta 8-27-72)']. One thing to know going in: the Jack Straw has a cut, which one reviewer called "heart breaking" given that this is literally Jack Straw being played in Wichita, Kansas [source: 'Jack Straw. Speaking of Jack Straw from Wichita! Massive cut, in the Wichita, Kansas version. Heart breaking'].
context: November 17, 1972, Century II Convention Hall, Wichita, Kansas — the tail end of a fall domestic run following the Europe '72 tour. The best circulating recording is a soundboard transfer by Mike Lai (identifier: gd1972-11-17.sbd.lai.1185.shnf), and one reviewer credited Lai's work directly, writing that "Mike Lai's excellent transfer brings this recording to full appreciation value" [source: 'Mike Lai\'s excellent transfer brings this recording to full appreciation value']. The show has not received an official release. The era — September through November 1972 — is one that at least one reviewer called "the most solid run" of the period, with many boards available and "so much inventive and evolutionary playing occurring" [source: 'so much inventive and evolutionary playing occurring'].
funFact: The Jack Straw cut prompted one reviewer to wonder if "there's another Wichita somewhere in the sky and the Jack Straw is played/recorded to perfection" [source: 'there\'s another Wichita somewhere in the sky and the Jack Straw is played/recorded to perfection'].
headline: Wichita, fall '72 — the second set earns the dig.
